title: Weapon Categories and Banks
text:
FreeSpace has two types of weapon banks: primary and secondary. There are three logical categories of weapon:

- **Primary weapons** (subtype "primary"): Lasers and ballistic cannons. They fire from primary weapon banks and typically have unlimited or ammo-based ammunition.

- **Secondary weapons** (subtype "secondary"): Missiles, bombs, and torpedoes. They fire from secondary weapon banks and consume finite ammunition.

- **Beam weapons** (subtype "beam"): High-energy directed beams. Although beams are a distinct category with unique mechanics, they are always mounted on primary weapon banks because there are only two physical bank types. In the game data, most beam weapons have the internal subtype WP_LASER (not WP_BEAM) because they are defined in the #Primary Weapons section of the table files. The `is_beam` property in tool responses is the reliable way to identify them.

title: Ship Types vs. Ship Classes
text: A **ship type** (e.g. "fighter", "bomber", "cruiser", "capital") is an abstract category that defines shared AI behaviors, targeting rules, and gameplay properties. A **ship class** (e.g. "GTF Ulysses", "GVF Serapis") is a specific model of ship with its own stats, weapons, and visual appearance. Each ship class belongs to exactly one ship type via its `class_type` field. Ship types control things like whether the AI will accept player orders, auto-attack enemies, attempt broadside maneuvers, or form into wings.

title: Species in FreeSpace
text:
Species define the major factions in the FreeSpace universe. The three canonical species are:

- **Terran**: Humanity, part of the Galactic Terran-Vasudan Alliance (GTVA).
- **Vasudan**: An alien race, allied with Terrans in the GTVA after the Terran-Vasudan War.
- **Shivan**: A mysterious and hostile alien race, the primary antagonist.

Each species has default settings for IFF (identification friend-or-foe), AWACS multipliers, countermeasures, and support ships. Mods may add additional species.

title: Names and Display Names
text:
A **name** is the canonical key for most FRED entities, including ships, ship classes, wings, jump nodes, and so forth. Names have a hard limit of 31 characters. Many entities also support a **display name**, which can be of unlimited length. If a display name is present, it is shown to the player during a mission instead of the canonical name. Names are used in SEXPs, table references, and internal lookups; display names are purely for player-facing presentation.

When a name is assigned to an entity, if the name contains a hash character `#`, a display name is automatically created from the characters preceding the hash. For example, a ship named `Corsair#2` would automatically get the display name `Corsair`. This allows multiple ships to share the same player-visible name while retaining unique canonical names. However, a double hash `##` disables the truncation — instead, the `##` is converted to a single `#` in the display name. For example, a name like `Alpha##1` would get the display name `Alpha#1`.

title: SEXP (S-Expression) System Overview
text:
**Syntax**: SEXPs are the scripting language used in FreeSpace mission events. They use a Lisp-like S-expression syntax where each expression is an operator followed by its arguments in parentheses.

**Discovery tools**: SEXPs are organized into categories and subcategories. Use `list_sexp_categories` to see all categories, their subcategories, and operator counts. Use `list_sexp_operators` to list operators, optionally filtered by category, subcategory, or name. Use `get_sexp_operator` to get full details of a specific operator. Use `get_sexp_argument_type` and `get_sexp_return_type` to understand what values each argument type accepts.

**Data types**: Although strings (and data derived from strings, such as ship names and weapon names) can be accepted as arguments to SEXPs, they cannot be returned from SEXPs. SEXP return types are limited to boolean, number, and void (action operators). Furthermore, the only numbers that can be represented in the SEXP system — whether as arguments or return values — are integers. If a mission needs to work with floating-point values, this must be done in a Lua script.

**Events**: Events use SEXPs as triggers and actions. A typical event has a conditional SEXP that, when true, fires one or more action SEXPs.

title: Mission File Structure
text:
A FreeSpace mission file (.fs2) contains several major sections:

- **Mission Info**: Metadata (name, author, description, flags)
- **Ships**: All ships placed in the mission with positions, orientations, loadouts, AI settings, and arrival/departure conditions
- **Wings**: Groups of ships that fly together in formation
- **Events**: SEXP-driven logic (triggers and actions)
- **Goals**: Mission objectives shown to the player
- **Waypoints**: Named paths for AI navigation
- **Messages**: In-mission dialogue with sender and audio
- **Briefing**: Pre-mission briefing stages with text and camera positions
- **Debriefing**: Post-mission stages triggered by conditions
- **Reinforcements**: Ships/wings that can be called in mid-mission

FRED2 is the mission editor. Missions are saved with the .fs2 extension.

title: IFF (Identification Friend or Foe)
text: IFF determines team allegiances in a mission. Standard IFF teams include "Friendly", "Hostile", and "Unknown", but custom IFF entries can be defined. Each IFF has an associated color for HUD display and relationships with other IFF teams (friend, foe, or neutral). Ships on the same IFF team are allies; ships on opposing IFF teams are enemies. SEXPs can change IFF allegiances mid-mission.

title: Ship Subsystems
text:
Subsystems are targetable components of a ship, each with their own hitpoints. Common subsystem types include:

- **Engines**: Disabling stops the ship from moving
- **Weapons**: Disabling prevents the ship from firing
- **Sensors/Communication**: Affects targeting and messaging
- **Navigation**: Affects autopilot and warp capability
- **Turrets**: Individual gun emplacements on larger ships
- **Fighter bays**: Launch and recovery bays for carried craft

Subsystems can be individually targeted, damaged, disabled, and repaired. Many SEXPs reference subsystems by name.

title: Weapon Banks and Allowed Weapons
text:
**Bank types**: Primary weapons and beams must be mounted on primary banks. Secondary weapons must be mounted on secondary banks. This is always enforced.

**Allowed weapons**: Each ship class can define which weapons are allowed on its banks, via a global allowed-weapons list and optional per-bank overrides. The `allowed_weapons` field lists weapons allowed in regular missions; `allowed_dogfight_weapons` lists weapons allowed in multiplayer dogfight missions. In most cases the regular and dogfight lists are the same, but they can differ. When a bank has per-bank restrictions (`restricted` is true), those override the global list for that bank. But per-bank allowed-weapons are always a subset of global allowed-weapons.

**Enforcement**: In practice, allowed weapons are only enforced for ships that belong to wings listed in Starting Wings. For other ships, the mission designer can assign any weapon with a compatible type (primary/secondary), but assigning a non-allowed weapon is considered a violation of the in-universe rules. Even for Starting Wings, allowed weapons are not enforced if the mission is a Scramble mission.

title: Lua Scripts and Dynamic SEXPs
text:
A mod can contain scripts written in Lua that provide additional functionality not coded into the game engine.

**Invocation**: Scripts can be invoked via game hooks (events fired by the engine at specific points, such as on game start, on ship death, on frame render, etc.) or evaluated by the script-eval SEXPs.

**Dynamic SEXPs**: It is also possible to call scripts from dynamic SEXPs. Dynamic SEXPs are provided by the mod rather than the game engine, by specifying SEXP operator metadata along with a script function. In terms of functionality and mission integration, they work just like built-in SEXPs.

title: Coordinate System and 3D Conventions
text:
FreeSpace uses a **left-handed coordinate system**:

- **+X** is right
- **+Y** is up
- **+Z** is forward

A ship's nose points along the +Z axis.

**Units**: All distances are in **meters**.

**Orientations** are represented as 3x3 rotation matrices consisting of three vectors in this order:
- **rvec** (right vector)
- **uvec** (up vector)
- **fvec** (forward vector)

An identity orientation (facing forward, no rotation) is: rvec=(1,0,0) uvec=(0,1,0) fvec=(0,0,1).

**Euler angles**: heading (h) = rotation around the Y axis, bank (b) = rotation around the Z axis, pitch (p) = rotation around the X axis. Rotations are applied in the order heading, bank, pitch (i.e. Y, Z, X). Strictly speaking these are Tait-Bryan angles, not classic Euler angles.

**Model bounding boxes**: The `mins` and `maxs` fields from `get_ship_model_details` define the bounding box of the ship model in the model's local coordinate frame. For example, `maxs.z - mins.z` gives the overall length of the ship.

title: Subsystem Name Comparison
text:
**Important**: Subsystem names must NEVER be compared using common string comparison. The game engine allows minor variations in subsystem names (such as the presence or absence of a trailing 's'), so two names that look different as strings may refer to the same subsystem.

Always use the `subsystem_names_equal` tool to check if two subsystem names match, or `subsystem_names_compare` for ordering. These tools use the engine's internal comparison function that correctly handles all allowable variations.

title: Operation Timeouts and Modal Dialogs
text:
FRED2 has many modal dialogs that require user input. It is possible for an MCP operation to be interrupted by a dialog, causing the operation to time out.

If an operation times out, use get_ui_status to check for modal dialogs. If a modal dialog is active, ask the user to address it before retrying the operation. If no modal dialog is active, the operation may simply need more time — use set_timeout to increase the timeout and try again.

Most operations complete quickly, in less than a second. Operations requiring a model load may take longer. Since a mission load involves loading many models, the load_mission operation typically takes the longest. The default timeout is 10 seconds.

Use get_timeout to read the current timeout value and set_timeout to change it.

title: Unicode and Character Encoding
text: Use `get_server_info` to check the `supports_unicode` field before passing text to FRED. If `supports_unicode` is false, all strings passed to FRED — including ship names, message text, briefing text, and any other text fields — must contain only characters in the standard ASCII range [0–127]. Characters outside this range (accented letters, non-Latin scripts, emoji, etc.) must NOT be used, as they may corrupt data or cause undefined behavior.

title: Special Tokens in Mission Text
text:
Mission text (briefings, debriefings, command briefings, messages, subtitles) can contain special tokens that are replaced at runtime.

**Localization tokens**:
- `$callsign` → player's callsign
- `$rank` → player's rank display name
- `$rtitle` → player's rank title (abbreviated)
- `$quote` → double quote character `"`
- `$semicolon` → semicolon `;`
- `$slash` → forward slash `/`
- `$backslash` → backslash `\`

**Briefing color tags**:
The `$` character is the meta character for color markup. Color markup can only be used in command briefings, briefings, and debriefings. Format: `$c` followed by whitespace for a color code that affects only the subsequent word (including any punctuation trailing that word), or `$c{...}$}` for a color code that affects a span of text, where `c` is a single letter indicating a color. Standard tags include `$w` (white), `$r` (red), `$g` (green), `$b` (blue), `$y` (yellow), `$o` (orange), `$f` (friendly green), `$h` (hostile red), `$n` (neutral), and uppercase variants for bright colors (`$W`, `$R`, `$G`, `$B`, `$Y`). Additional tags: `$k` (black), `$e`/`$E` (grey), `$v`/`$V` (violet), `$p`/`$P` (pink). Special sequences: `$}` closes a color span, `$|` stops coloring and can be used to prevent color from affecting trailing punctuation. Custom colors can be added via colors.tbl.

**Key remapping tokens**:
Format: `$keyname$` (with trailing `$`). Replaced with the player's actual key binding, if a control uses that key by default. Example: `$Tab$` becomes the key bound to the afterburner, whose default key is Tab.

**SEXP data substitutions**:
SEXP variable names with a leading `$` and SEXP container references with a leading `&` are replaced with their current values. Example: $result is replaced by the value of the variable `result`, if a variable with that exact name exists.
